note: EvoTune (Surina, Mansouri, Quaedvlieg, Seddas, Viazovska, Abbe, Gulcehre; EPFL/Apple; arXiv:2504.05108v4, 4 Aug 2025). Algorithm-discovery method that tightly couples LLM-guided program generation + FunSearch-style island-based evolutionary search + RL (off-policy DPO with forward-KL) in one alternating loop. The loop is closed EMPIRICALLY: every generated Python program (a priority/heuristic function) is executed and scored on held-out benchmark instances (optimality gap / NMSE), and those scores are the reward driving both the program database and the RL fine-tuning. Benchmarks: online bin packing, TSP (via GLS), flatpack, Google Hash Code (Datacenter, Self-Driving), LLM-SR symbolic regression, and TSPLib; main results averaged over 10 seeds. Consistently beats a FunSearch-only baseline. Notable scope/boundary facts relevant to the manuscript: (a) paper EXPLICITLY disclaims SOTA and disclaims being a general system -- tasks are 'testbeds' and it uses only small open LLMs (Llama3.2 1B, Phi-3.5 Mini 3.8B, Granite 3.1 2B); (b) it beats the top human team on Hash Code Datacenter (418 > 407) but does NOT beat humans on Hash Code Self-Driving within budget; (c) the paper's own stated limitations are model-size/sampling-budget SCALING and RL COMPUTE cost -- it does NOT itself name interpretability as a bottleneck, though its evolved heuristics (Listings 1-3, e.g. an 18-hyperparameter bin-packing priority() function) are conspicuously opaque, which is consistent with the manuscript's interpretability characterization.

C094.evidence: Manuscript L176 (BG, domain map): '...and CS/algorithms~\citep{real2020automlzero,surina2025algodiscovery,jaber2026autokernel}.' surina2025algodiscovery is a grouped member of the CS/algorithms domain, which is exactly its subject. Paper title: 'Algorithm Discovery With LLMs: Evolutionary Search Meets Reinforcement Learning' (p.1); abstract frames it as 'accelerating the discovery of algorithms across various domains' and studies 'combinatorial optimization tasks' (Abstract, L10-21). Tasks are all CS/algorithmic (bin packing, TSP, flatpack, Hash Code competitive programming, symbolic regression). Domain placement is exact; grouped background/domain-list citation is fully supportable.

C095.evidence: Manuscript L450 (CHAR): 'Computer science / algorithms ($V_\text{emp}$; fully closed). AutoML-Zero~..., algorithm discovery~\citep{surina2025algodiscovery}, and AutoKernel~... close the loop via reliable benchmark/empirical validation and evolutionary or LLM-guided search. Validation is high-reliability and the loop is fully closed; the residual bottleneck is interpretability---the discovered artifacts work but resist human understanding, a limitation of $M$ and output form rather than of $V$.' Every surina-relevant attribute is confirmed by the paper. (1) 'algorithm discovery' -- title-level; 'discover improved algorithms' (Abstract, L17). (2) 'evolutionary or LLM-guided search' -- 'we propose to augment LLM-based evolutionary search by continuously refining the search operator - the LLM - through reinforcement learning (RL) fine-tuning' (Abstract, L15-17); FunSearch-style island-based database (Sec 3.1, L253-256). (3) 'close the loop via reliable benchmark/empirical validation' ($V_\text{emp}$) -- programs are EXECUTED and scored on held-out instances, which is the reward closing the loop: 'Each generated program is evaluated on a predefined set of validation task instances. Programs that are successfully evaluated without errors or exceeding computational constraints are assigned a score based on their performance' (Sec 3.1, L248-252); Fig 1 caption 'Python programs generated by an LLM are evaluated on a set of combinatorial optimization problem instances and then stored in a program database' (L90-92). (4) 'high-reliability' validation -- deterministic program execution measured by optimality gap, 'For all tasks, performance is measured with the optimality gap' (L354), 'each reported result is the average of ten random seeds' (L373-374). The loop-closure + empirical-validation + LLM-guided/evolutionary-search characterization is a faithful match. The 'residual bottleneck is interpretability' clause is the MANUSCRIPT'S OWN $V/M$-framework synthesis across the three domain works (not a self-claim by surina), and it is consistent with -- not contradicted by -- the paper's opaque evolved heuristics (Listings 1-3), so it does not undermine the citation. See reverse_findings for scope nuances (none rise to a defect).
C095.fix: None required. (Optional, LOW/info: the 'residual bottleneck is interpretability' framing is the manuscript's synthesis, not surina's self-described limitation -- the paper's own stated limitations are model/budget scaling and RL compute cost. Since interpretability is asserted as the manuscript's domain-level $V/M$ judgment and is well-illustrated by surina's Listings 1-3, no change is needed.)

reverse_findings.fact: Paper explicitly scopes itself to combinatorial optimization + symbolic regression and EXPLICITLY disclaims state-of-the-art / general-purpose claims.
reverse_findings.loc: paper App A.12 (surina.txt L3629-3632): 'The primary goal of our work was not to achieve state-of-the-art performance on specific benchmarks, especially as we use smaller, open-source LLMs, but rather to use these tasks as testbeds for rigorously evaluating the effectiveness of our proposed EvoTune method'; vs manuscript L176/L450 ('CS/algorithms', 'algorithm discovery')
reverse_findings.mismatch: The manuscript's domain labels ('CS/algorithms', 'algorithm discovery') read broadly, while the paper is narrowly a combinatorial-optimization + symbolic-regression heuristic-discovery method using small open LLMs and NOT aiming for SOTA. This does NOT affect either claim: C094 is a domain-membership cite (correct) and C095 attributes only loop-closure + empirical-validation + LLM/evolutionary search (all hold within the paper's actual scope). Recorded as scope context only.

reverse_findings.fact: EvoTune beats humans on some benchmarks but not others: surpasses the top human team on Hash Code Datacenter (418 vs 407) but does NOT beat the best human solution on Hash Code Self-Driving within budget.
reverse_findings.loc: paper Sec 4.2 (surina.txt L1006-1008: '...EvoTune achieves a score of 418 surpassing the competition's top human score of 407'); App A.9 (L1884-1886: 'on Self-Driving task, neither EvoTune or FunSearch do not outperform the best solution found by human teams within the sampling budget of 20,000 functions')
reverse_findings.mismatch: No conflict with the manuscript. C095 claims loop-closure + reliable empirical validation, NOT superhuman/SOTA performance; loop closure holds regardless of whether a given run beats humans. Recorded for completeness in case any reviewer reads 'fully closed / high-reliability' as implying uniformly superhuman results (it does not).

reverse_findings.fact: The paper's own stated limitations are model-size / sampling-budget SCALING and RL COMPUTE cost; it does NOT itself identify interpretability as a bottleneck.
reverse_findings.loc: paper Sec 6 Conclusion (surina.txt L1069-1076): 'Our experiments were conducted with LLMs ranging from 1B to 3.8B parameters and with a sampling budget of up to 22.4k outputs. Further scaling ... is needed' and '...it incurs additional compute costs due to the RL training phase'; vs manuscript L450 'the residual bottleneck is interpretability'
reverse_findings.mismatch: The manuscript's 'residual bottleneck is interpretability' is an external $V/M$-framework synthesis, not a claim the paper makes about itself. It is nonetheless SUPPORTED (not contradicted) by the paper's evolved artifacts -- e.g. the best bin-packing heuristic (Listing 1) is an ~18-hyperparameter dense numpy priority() function that resists human understanding while achieving a strong optimality gap (2.06). Low-severity nuance: the interpretability judgment is the manuscript's, applied at the domain level; surina supplies the opaque-but-working artifacts, not the diagnosis.

reverse_findings.fact: 'LLM-guided search' in the manuscript understates the method: surina's core novelty is adding RL (off-policy DPO) IN THE LOOP on top of LLM-guided evolutionary search, distinguishing it from static-generator FunSearch-style methods.
reverse_findings.loc: paper Abstract/Sec 1 contributions (surina.txt L118-123): 'To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to demonstrate the potential of tightly integrating LLM-based evolutionary search with RL in the loop'
reverse_findings.mismatch: The manuscript's 'evolutionary or LLM-guided search' descriptor is accurate but omits the RL-in-the-loop element that is surina's headline contribution. Not a defect -- the manuscript's point (empirical loop-closure via evolutionary/LLM-guided search) holds and does not depend on the RL detail; RL only strengthens the 'loop is closed / self-improving' reading. Recorded for completeness.
